narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared strong aspects of your background, including a solid LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—and coming from a high family income bracket, which many students don't have. Your undergraduate GPA sits at 3.10, close to the average of 3.21, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 48.7%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lowest group compared to peers. This outweighed your LSAT strength, which was the second-most influential factor and pushed notably in the favorable direction toward passing, thanks to that above-average score. Your high family income also helped by nudging the prediction toward success, as it correlates with better outcomes in the model's view of past students. Overall, your lower Year 3 deciles stood out as the dominant concern, especially since they deviated more sharply from typical strong performers who rank around the middle or higher. While your LSAT and background provided a boost, they weren't enough to offset that key academic signal in this competitive process. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings, as schools value consistent upward trends. You might also gather letters highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic student overcoming challenges, or explore post-bac programs to strengthen your profile. Reapplying after addressing that Year 3 performance could shift your predicted odds significantly. Keep pushing—many in similar situations turn it around with targeted improvements.
